title: Ground state correlations in the one-dimensional Fermi one-component plasma
abstract: Structural and dynamic correlations in the ground state of the one-dimensional Fermi one-component plasma are studied by Quantum Monte Carlo simulations. Results are presented for the pair correlation function, static structure factor, the one-particle density matrix and the momentum distribution, for the cases of full, partial and no polarization. Evidence is reported of density correlations slowly decaying with distance, with the concurrent emergence of quasi-crystalline order even in the weakly correlated regime. Effects of quantum statistics in the momentum distribution are discussed.
summary: This paper uses Quantum Monte Carlo simulations to study the ground state properties of a 1D electron gas. It demonstrates that long-range Coulomb interactions lead to quasi-crystalline order and shows how spin polarization affects the visibility of quantum statistics in the momentum distribution.
main_problem: The study investigates structural and dynamic correlations in the ground state of a one-dimensional Fermi one-component plasma, specifically testing for deviations from Tomonaga-Luttinger Liquid Theory due to long-range Coulomb interactions.
main_result: The researchers found evidence of quasi-crystalline order emerging even in weakly correlated regimes and observed that quantum statistics significantly influence the momentum distribution in partially polarized systems, while being suppressed in unpolarized systems.
method: Unbiased Quantum Monte Carlo simulations using the canonical variant of the continuous-space Worm Algorithm, with results extrapolated to the ground state limit.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional assembly of N identical spin-1/2 charged particles in a box of length L with periodic boundary conditions, neutralized by a uniform background of opposite charge (jellium/OCP model) with repulsive Coulomb interactions.
key_observables: Pair correlation function, static structure factor, one-particle density matrix, momentum distribution, and energy per particle.
important_parameters: Dimensionless density parameter (rs) ranging from 1 to 20, system polarization (zeta) ranging from fully polarized to unpolarized, and the LT scaling property.
main_assumptions: The 1D nature of the system allows for the avoidance of the sign problem; the study assumes the validity of the LT scaling for data collapsing despite potential long-range interaction deviations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows energy per particle vs temperature for different rs values; Figure 7 displays momentum distributions for majority and minority spin components in partially polarized systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 1D OCP model and the scientific problem, describes the Worm Algorithm QMC methodology, presents validation against existing literature, details structural and one-body results across different density and polarization regimes, and concludes with a discussion on quantum statistics and TLL theory.
why_interesting: The study provides deep insights into how long-range interactions and spin polarization influence many-body correlations and the emergence of quasi-crystalline order, which is fundamental to understanding 1D many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Energy per particle as a function of temperature (both quantities in Hartree) for a fully polarized system of electrons and two different values of rs. The numbers of par- ticles are 20 (highest temperature), 40 and 80 (lowest temper- ature). Horizontal dashed lines are fits to the data. Statistical errors are smaller than symbol sizes.

FIG. 2. Pair correlation function g(x) computed for a fully polarized (ζ = 1) system with rs = 2 at two temperatures (in Ha) and with system sizes so that the product LT remains constant. The data are observed to collapse (statistical errors are of the order of the symbol size).

Fig. 2 displays the pair correlation function g(x) for a fully polarized system with rs = 2, plotted as a func- tion of x/rs. Within the statistical uncertainties of our calculation, collapse of the data is observed; just like for the energy, we find that to be the case for all values of rs considered here. Fig. 3 shows the so determined ground state pair correlation functions for a fully po- larized (i.e., ζ = 1) system of varying rs, specifically
FIG. 3. Ground state pair correlation function g(x) computed for a polarized system with rs = 1, 2, 5, 10, 20. Curves with higher peaks correspond to greater values of rs.

FIG. 4. Static structure factor S(q) computed for a fully polarized (ζ = 1) system with rs = 20 (top) and rs = 2 (bottom). Data are plotted as a function of q/qF , where qF = π/(2rs). Statistical errors are smaller than symbol size. The data shown pertain to three different system sizes and temperatures. The lowest temperature is 3.125 × 10−2 Ha, and the product LT is the same for all systems.
